Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of school, so I'd mostly just fit studying in whenever I could. I'd listen to lectures while commuting or doing chores since I learn better by hearing things anyway. Some weeks I'd put in maybe 15 hours, other weeks barely anything. It was pretty scattered, and I know that's not ideal, but it's just how things worked out for me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what else was happening in my life. Deadlines were really the main thing that would kick me into gear. I'd go weeks without doing much, then suddenly realize something was due and cram. It wasn't regular at all - more like bursts of activity followed by long stretches of nothing. I kept telling myself I'd get more consistent, but it never really happened.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which honestly happened more than I'd like to admit. The peer support group would sometimes remind me to check in too, which helped. But I wasn't someone who logged in out of curiosity or genuine interest - it was more reactive than proactive.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the peer support thing got me connected with a few other students. That made me feel a bit more accountable. But it never became a true habit. I'd have a good week or two, then drift off again. The LMS just never felt like a natural part of my day.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them while doing other things, which fit my lifestyle. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too when people actually engaged - hearing different perspectives on the material helped it click. I also appreciated when instructors would post audio summaries or explanations. Anything I could listen to worked better than reading walls of text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. I'd download them thinking I'd get to them later, but they'd just sit in a folder. The optional practice quizzes I rarely touched either - they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics. If it wasn't going to be graded or wasn't in audio format, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments, it felt worth my time. Also, anything that other students in my peer group said was helpful - that social proof mattered to me. I needed to feel like the activity would actually pay off, not just be busywork. At my age, I don't have time to waste on things that don't move the needle.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, my brain just categorized it as 'nice to have' and then I'd never get to it. Required stuff had consequences, so I'd eventually do it, even if last minute. Optional things felt like they were for students who had their lives together, which wasn't me. The distinction really affected how I prioritized my limited time.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded a lecture or PDF, I'd often work with it offline. I'd listen to audio on my phone during my commute or while cooking. I also used some external YouTube videos to understand concepts better - sometimes random educators explain things more clearly. My actual learning happened a lot outside the LMS, just using materials I'd grabbed from it.
Q10: There were many times I'd be replaying a downloaded lecture multiple times, taking notes on paper, really trying to understand something - but none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be discussing concepts with a friend over coffee, working through problems together. The LMS only saw me download something once, not the hours I spent with it afterward. My studying was often invisible to the system.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it, and then that was my copy to work with. I didn't need to keep going back to the LMS for the same material. It was more convenient to have everything on my phone or laptop where I could access it without internet. So my engagement looked minimal online, but I was actually using those materials repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when the workload piled up and I felt behind. The anxiety would creep in, and paradoxically that made me avoid the course more. I'd feel overwhelmed looking at all the things I'd missed, so I'd just... not look. It became this avoidance cycle. Life stuff would also interfere - work obligations, family things. Being 28 means you can't just focus on school alone.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed from other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Confusion was a big factor too - if I didn't understand something, I'd sometimes just close the tab instead of pushing through. Competing responsibilities were constant. I'm not a traditional student with nothing else going on, so the course often had to take a backseat to more immediate demands.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning. It was performative engagement, if that makes sense - present but not really there.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but still put it off because I felt unprepared or anxious about it. The bigger or more complex something seemed, the more I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was in a better headspace, but tomorrow would come and I'd feel the same way. It's a bad habit I've struggled with for years.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd be dormant for weeks, then suddenly very active right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated. The pressure of a deadline would cut through all my avoidance and force me to engage. Without deadlines, I probably would have done even less than I did. They were stressful but necessary for me.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it did motivate me to engage more. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback promptly - sometimes I'd avoid it out of anxiety about what it might say. When the peer support group would discuss feedback together, that helped me actually process it. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could do this; critical feedback sometimes made me want to hide.
Q18: The structure felt a bit overwhelming with 19 courses to juggle. I couldn't keep track of everything, so I'd focus on whatever seemed most urgent. A more streamlined structure might have helped me stay engaged. The peer support intervention was actually helpful in giving me some external structure and accountability that I lacked on my own. Without that, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder - I'd see how much there was and feel defeated before starting. But having video content was good for my learning style. The discussion forums could be helpful when active, but sometimes they felt dead. Clearer pathways through the material would have helped - I often felt lost about what to prioritize. The peer matching was one thing that actually made engagement easier.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious or unsure, checking things repeatedly without committing to actually doing the work. Or maybe they're trying to understand what's expected but struggling to execute. It could also be someone like me who opens things, gets overwhelmed, and closes them without completing anything. High activity doesn't mean productive activity - sometimes it's just anxious browsing.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I got a Merit despite my low attendance and moderate assignment completion because I could perform when it counted - exams especially. I'd absorb material through listening and then do okay on tests even if my day-to-day engagement looked poor. Some people just learn differently or have life circumstances that limit their visible participation but not their actual understanding.
Q22: Logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. That's useful for identifying who might be struggling. But they miss so much - offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, emotional state, life circumstances. They can't tell if someone's truly learning or just going through motions. They're a partial picture at best, and can be misleading at worst.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about and thinking about the material, not just completing tasks. It's when concepts stick with you beyond the assignment, when you find yourself connecting ideas to real life. It's not about how many times you logged in or how long you watched videos. True engagement is mental and emotional investment in learning, which doesn't always show up in metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how the peer support actually helped me feel less alone in struggling, or how much I learned from offline conversations with other students. It wouldn't capture the anxiety that kept me away sometimes, or the genuine interest I had in certain topics despite my poor participation stats. It can't see that I'm a 28-year-old trying to balance multiple life demands, doing my best even when my best looks inadequate on paper.
